text: The surgeon begins dissection on the ipsilateral side in the left lower region, using a Foley balloon catheter to create space in the preperitoneal area over the arcuate line before docking the robot.

The surgeon completes the ipsilateral dissection and begins the crossover technique, reducing the insufflation pressure to twelve millimeters of mercury to enhance dissection and minimize the risk of creating holes. The medial dissection of the ipsilateral retroactive space is visible, providing access to the posterior sheath for the crossover maneuver.

The surgeon completes the retroactive dissection on the contralateral side, identifying the neurovascular bundles. The dissection extends superiorly and inferiorly to ensure adequate space for mesh overlap.

After reducing all hernias, the surgeon inspects for bleeding and closes any posterior holes created during dissection. A V-lock suture is used for larger defects, while a Vicryl suture is applied for smaller ones with good tissue structure. Posterior components are utilized to assist in closure.

The surgeon inspects the intra-abdominal area after placing the mesh, checking for any abnormalities, injuries, or bleeding. Fiber and glue are applied to the region, and the mesh appears intact. The surgeon examines the left side, observes adhesions, and performs a crossover inspection to the opposite side, confirming that everything looks normal.
The surgeon performs a tap block in the right lower quadrant, observing the area for any signs of inflammation, injury, or abnormalities. The cecum appears normal with no visible issues.
The surgeon performs an exploratory procedure, opening the posterior sheath to evaluate the mesh for signs of infection or abnormalities within the intra-abdominal region.

The surgeon enters through the left upper quadrant at the subcostal port site and places robotic working ports laterally to the mesh. The intra-abdominal evaluation reveals the abdomen is free of active bile or purulence, but a contained abscess is visible in the right lower quadrant. The posterior sheath appears intact with no breakdown or fistula communication. The surgeon takes down adhesions, opens the posterior sheath, and evaluates the mesh directly, revealing a standard small hematoma without purulence or abnormalities.

The surgeon has fully opened the posterior sheath, revealing no signs of a fistula or abscess cause beneath it. The focus shifts to the right lower quadrant, suspecting stump appendicitis and abscess. The surgeon closes the posterior sheath after decompressing a small hematoma, irrigates the area, and closes the rent made. Due to poor tissue quality, a rotational flap using Felsiform tissue is planned to close the posterior defect.
The surgeon places a drain into the region of the small hematoma prior to closing the defect, aiming to further drain the hematoma and evaluate for any purulence.

The surgeon immobilizes the falciform ligament and surrounding structures, leaving a portion intact to maintain blood supply. The surgeon then assesses the most suitable location for covering the defect.

The surgeon uses a barbed suture to close the space, ensuring thorough coverage of the area. After verifying no infection in the posterior sheath, the surgeon considers drainage options to avoid contamination of the mesh, preferring lateral drainage away from the mesh.

The surgeon places an intra-abdominal drain outside the abscess cavity to collect potential contamination and applies fibrin glue to the closure site to enhance the seal, preventing any purulent contents from accessing the retroactive space.
The surgeon connects insufflation to the intra-abdominal drain, causing the abdomen to inflate. This allows access using the OptiView port for the procedure.

The posterior sheath remains intact, with the omentum adhered above it. The drain shows no signs of external contamination. The surgeon proceeds to address the right lower quadrant abscess, performing focal drainage in that area.

The surgeon uses a combination of a vessel sealer, suction, and kidney resectors to access an abscess cavity, which appears to be related to a perforated appendix. The cavity is located in the retro mesenteric field and extends to the right lower quadrant, filled with air, consistent with the suspected perforated appendicitis.

The surgeon encounters a phlegmon with purulent material in the right lower quadrant, using kidney resectors for dissection. The fluid collection is opened up through general dissection and retraction, revealing its lateral position relative to the retroactive space.

The surgeon carefully examines the intra-abdominal area to ensure complete dissection of the abscess cavity, checking for any remaining inter-loop abscesses that might require further surgical intervention.

The surgeon irrigates the abscess cavity copiously with antibiotic irrigation fluid, leaving a drain in the right lower quadrant extending into the pelvis and another in the open space visible in the surgical field. The surrounding tissue shows some phlegmon and exudate, but the majority of the abscess has been drained.
